text: What is the central processing unit (CPU) often called?
fact: The CPU handles all instructions from your computer.

text: What does AI (Artificial Intelligence) help computers do?
fact: AI allows machines to learn from experience and adjust to new inputs.

text: How does a touch screen sense your finger?
fact: Capacitive touch screens use the electrical charge of your skin to work.

text: What is the global network that connects computers worldwide?
fact: The Internet is a massive network of networks that connects billions of devices.

text: What type of robot is used to fly and take aerial photos?
fact: Drones use multiple rotors to fly and stay very stable for cameras.

text: Which part of the computer stores files permanently?
fact: SSDs store your pictures, games, and os even when the power is off.

text: What language do computer chips speak natively?
fact: Computers only understand 0s and 1s, which represent 'Off' and 'On'.

text: What is a 'Bug' in computer coding?
fact: Bugs are mistakes that make software behave in unexpected ways.

text: What part of a robot acts like its 'eyes'?
fact: Cameras and light sensors help robots 'see' the world around them.

text: What technology allows you to wear special glasses to see a virtual world?
fact: VR creates a computer-generated environment with scenes that appear to be real.

text: Which planet is the fourth from the sun and is called the 'Red Planet'?
fact: Mars is red because its surface is covered in iron oxide (rust).

text: What force keeps us on the ground and causes objects to fall?
fact: Everything with mass has gravity, which pulls things toward each other.

text: What is the primary gas we breathe in to survive?
fact: Our bodies use oxygen to break down food and create energy.

text: What process do plants use to make food from sunlight?
fact: Plants take in CO2 and release Oxygen while making sugar from light.

text: What is the largest animal to ever live on Earth?
fact: The Blue Whale can grow up to 100 feet long and weigh as much as 30 elephants.

text: What are the building blocks of everything in the universe?
fact: Everything you touch is made of tiny atoms too small to see with your eyes.

text: What part of the cell holds the 'instructions' for life (DNA)?
fact: The nucleus acts like the control center for the whole cell.

text: What happens to water when it reaches 100 degrees Celsius?
fact: At 100°C, water turns from liquid into gas (steam).

text: Which star provides heat and light to our solar system?
fact: The Sun is a medium-sized star that holds 99% of our solar system's mass.

text: How many planets are currently in our solar system?
fact: Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.

text: What does a solar panel do?
fact: Solar panels capture photons and convert them into electrical power.

text: Which of these is a fast-growing plant that pandas love to eat?
fact: Bamboo can grow over 3 feet in a single day!

text: What do we call the path Earth takes around the Sun?
fact: It takes 365 days for the Earth to complete one full orbit.

text: What protects the Earth from the Sun's harmful UV rays?
fact: The ozone layer is a gas shield in the upper atmosphere.

text: What kind of animal is a frog?
fact: Amphibians usually spend part of their life in water and part on land.

text: Which human organ filters blood and is shaped like a bean?
fact: You have two kidneys that help clean your body's fluids.

text: What is the hardest natural mineral found on Earth?
fact: Diamonds are made of carbon atoms packed extremely tight.

text: Which part of a plant absorbs water from the soil?
fact: Roots also help anchor the plant so it doesn't blow away.

text: What is the boiling point of water in Fahrenheit?
fact: In the Fahrenheit system, water boils at 212 degrees.

text: What does 'Wi-Fi' stand for?
fact: Wi-Fi is technology for radio wireless local area networking of devices.

text: What do we call the tiny organisms that can cause sickness?
fact: While some bacteria make us sick, many are actually helpful!

text: What color is the sky on Mars?
fact: Dust in the Martian atmosphere gives the sky a butterscotch tint.

text: Which bird is known for being able to fly backwards?
fact: A hummingbird's wings move in a figure-eight pattern.

text: What is the state of matter of a rock?
fact: Solids have a fixed shape and volume.

text: What is the molten rock inside a volcano called?
fact: It's called magma when underground, and lava when it erupts.

text: Which ocean is the largest on Earth?
fact: The Pacific covers about one-third of the entire surface of the Earth.

text: What do we call a scientist who studies stars and space?
fact: Astronomers use telescopes and math to understand the universe.

text: What is the name of our galaxy?
fact: Our solar system is located in one of the spiral arms of the Milky Way.

text: How many legs does an insect usually have?
fact: Spiders have 8 legs, but insects always have 6.

text: What is the main ingredient in glass?
fact: Glass is made by melting sand at very high temperatures.

text: What do we call the electricity that makes your hair stand up?
fact: Static electricity is the buildup of an electrical charge on an object's surface.

text: Which part of the body is responsible for pumping blood?
fact: Your heart is a strong muscle that works non-stop.

text: What gas do plants need to take in for photosynthesis?
fact: Plants breathe in CO2 that humans and animals breathe out.

text: What is the fastest land animal?
fact: A cheetah can reach speeds up to 70 miles per hour.

text: Which of these is used to measure temperature?
fact: Thermometers can use liquid or digital sensors to find heat levels.

text: What is the closest planet to the Sun?
fact: Mercury is very small and has almost no atmosphere.

text: What do we call the sound made by a lightning bolt?
fact: Thunder is the sound of the air expanding rapidly due to heat.

text: What is 'Coding' in simple terms?
fact: Code tells a computer exactly what to do step by step.

text: Who was the first person to walk on the moon?
fact: He famously said, 'One small step for man, one giant leap for mankind.'

text: What part of the tree tells us how old it is?
fact: Each ring usually represents one year of growth.

text: What has a face and two hands but no arms or legs?
fact: Clocks show time with their hands and faces.

text: What has many keys but can’t open a single lock?
fact: A piano has 88 keys, but they only make music.

text: What month of the year has 28 days?
fact: Every single month has at least 28 days!

text: I’m tall when I’m young, and I’m short when I’m old. What am I?
fact: Candles get shorter as the wax melts away.

text: What has to be broken before you can use it?
fact: You have to crack the shell to cook or eat an egg.

text: What can you catch but not throw?
fact: You catch a sickness, but it isn't an object you can throw.

text: What goes up but never comes down?
fact: Time only moves forward, so you keep getting older.

text: What gets wet while drying?
fact: A towel dries you and absorbs your water, becoming wet.

text: What belongs to you, but others use it more than you do?
fact: Other people call you by your name more than you say it.

text: What can travel around the world while staying in a corner?
fact: A stamp stays in the corner of an envelope as it travels in the mail.

text: What has cities, but no houses; mountains, but no trees; and water, but no fish?
fact: A map shows where things are without the real objects.

text: I have branches, but no fruit, trunk, or leaves. What am I?
fact: Banks have offices in different locations called branches.

text: What has a neck but no head?
fact: Bottles, shirts, and guitars all have a part called a 'neck'.

text: The more of this there is, the less you see. What is it?
fact: In total darkness, your eyes can't see anything.

text: I’m always in front of you but can’t be seen. What am I?
fact: The future hasn't happened yet, so it stays ahead of you.

text: What follows you all day long but disappears at night?
fact: Shadows need light to exist, so they vanish in the dark.

text: What has one eye but can’t see anything at all?
fact: A needle has an 'eye' for thread, and storms and potatoes have parts called 'eyes'.

text: Which word is spelled wrongly in every single dictionary?
fact: The word 'Wrongly' is always spelled W-R-O-N-G-L-Y.

text: What can you hold in your left hand but not in your right hand?
fact: You can't reach your right elbow with your right hand!

text: What starts with P, ends with E, and has thousands of letters?
fact: A Post Office handles thousands of mail letters every day.

text: What has hands but cannot clap?
fact: A clock's hands only point to the time.

text: What is so fragile that saying its name breaks it?
fact: The moment someone speaks, the silence is gone.

text: What has a thumb and four fingers but is not alive?
fact: A glove has the shape of a hand but no life.

text: Which weighs more: a pound of feathers or a pound of iron?
fact: A pound is a pound, no matter what material it is!

text: If an electric train is moving north, which way is the smoke blowing?
fact: Electric trains don't produce any smoke.

text: What word becomes shorter when you add two letters to it?
fact: Adding 'er' to 'short' makes it 'shorter'.

text: What is full of holes but still holds water?
fact: Sponges are very porous and absorb liquid.

text: The more you take, the more you leave behind. What are they?
fact: As you walk, you leave your footprints behind you.

text: What has a head and a tail but no body?
fact: Most coins have a 'head' side and a 'tails' side.

text: What kind of room has no doors or windows?
fact: A mushroom is a fungus, not a real room.

text: What stays where it is even after it goes out?
fact: A light fixture stays on the wall even when turned off.

text: What has a bed but never sleeps?
fact: A river flows in a channel called a riverbed.

text: What has a mouth but never eats?
fact: The end of a river is called its mouth.

text: I have one head, one foot, and four legs. What am I?
fact: A bed has a headboard, a footboard, and four support legs.

text: What has 13 hearts but no other organs?
fact: A standard deck has a 'Suit of Hearts' with 13 cards.

text: What can you break without even touching it?
fact: You break a promise by not doing what you said.

text: What goes around the entire yard without ever moving?
fact: A fence surrounds the yard but stays in the same place.

text: What has a thumb but no fingers?
fact: Mittens keep fingers together and have a separate spot for the thumb.

text: What has 4 eyes but can't see anything?
fact: The name Mississippi has four 'i's in it.

text: What follows you everywhere but is completely invisible?
fact: Your history and memories are always with you.

text: What is at the end of a rainbow?
fact: The very last letter of the word 'Rainbow' is 'W'.

text: What can you lose and still keep at the same time?
fact: When you 'lose' your temper, you still have the emotion!

text: What has words, but never speaks a sound?
fact: Books tell stories through written words, not voices.

text: If you drop a yellow hat in the Blue Sea, what does it become?
fact: No matter the color, the water makes it wet.

text: What has a spine but no bones inside?
fact: The binding of a book is called its spine.

text: What starts with T, ends with T, and has T in it?
fact: T-E-A-P-O-T!

text: What has 88 keys but cannot open any door?
fact: Musical keys make sounds, not unlock things.

text: What goes up and down but stays in the same place?
fact: Stairs lead up and down but don't move themselves.

text: What can travel through water but never get wet?
fact: Light reflecting on water isn't affected by the wetness.

text: What has a heart that doesn't beat?
fact: The center of an artichoke is called its 'heart'.

text: DB_VERSION_V10_STABLE
